name: Zhuge Liang
description: Zhuge Liang, also known as Kongming, is a pivotal figure in Chinese history and culture, particularly revered in the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 AD). While not a deity in the traditional sense like a god or goddess, Zhuge Liang is deified through popular veneration and is considered a semi-divine figure embodying wisdom, strategy, and loyalty. He served as the Chancellor and regent of the state of Shu Han. His brilliance as a military strategist, inventor, and statesman elevated him to legendary status. He is often depicted as a wise and benevolent advisor, possessing almost supernatural foresight and skill. His contributions to military tactics, such as the 'Eight Trigrams Formation,' and his unwavering dedication to restoring the Han Dynasty cemented his place in the pantheon of Chinese heroes. His influence extends beyond military strategy, influencing Chinese philosophy, literature, and popular culture. Zhuge Liang is often invoked for guidance and wisdom, blurring the lines between historical figure and divine inspiration.
subtitle: Reclining Dragon, Prime Minister of Shu

keyMyths.title: The Borrowing of Arrows
keyMyths.description: During a critical moment in the war against Cao Cao, Zhuge Liang ingeniously devised a plan to acquire arrows from the enemy. He sailed towards Cao Cao's fleet under the cover of fog, with straw-filled boats. Cao Cao, fearing an attack, ordered his archers to fire upon the approaching vessels, unknowingly providing Zhuge Liang with a vast supply of arrows. This displayed his cunning and resourcefulness in turning a disadvantage into a strategic advantage, highlighting his mastery of deception and psychological warfare.

keyMyths.title: The Empty Fort Strategy
keyMyths.description: Facing an overwhelming force led by Sima Yi, Zhuge Liang, with minimal troops, cleverly employed the Empty Fort Strategy. He ordered the gates of the city to be opened wide and sat calmly on the city walls playing the guqin. Sima Yi, suspecting an ambush, hesitated to attack, believing it to be a trap. This bold gamble demonstrated Zhuge Liang's confidence, psychological insight, and ability to manipulate his opponent's perceptions, securing a temporary victory.

keyMyths.title: The Wooden Oxen and Flowing Horses
keyMyths.description: To transport supplies across treacherous terrain during his Northern Expeditions, Zhuge Liang invented the 'Wooden Oxen and Flowing Horses,' ingenious mechanical devices that were essentially early forms of wheelbarrows or carts. These devices were designed to navigate difficult landscapes and carry heavy loads, showcasing Zhuge Liang's innovative thinking and his commitment to overcoming logistical challenges in his military campaigns. This invention significantly aided the Shu Han army's ability to sustain prolonged campaigns.

keyMyths.title: Predicting His Own Death
keyMyths.description: In the later years of his life, Zhuge Liang attempted to extend his lifespan through a ritual. However, the ritual was disrupted by Wei Yan, leading to Zhuge Liang's eventual death. This event is often interpreted as a demonstration of fate and the limitations of even the most brilliant minds. It highlights the tragic element of Zhuge Liang's story, showcasing his relentless pursuit of his goals even in the face of inevitable mortality.

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
